race.name: Maraton Franja BTC City
race.tagline: Europe's oldest gran fondo. 43 editions through the Slovenian heartland since 1982, named after a secret WWII partisan hospital hidden in a mountain gorge.

vitals.registration: Open registration via franja.org. Early bird EUR 44 (until March 31), regular EUR 55 (April 1 - May 15). No lottery. UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier.
vitals.prize_purse: None (timed event, UCI age-group qualifying, finisher medals)
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple refreshment stations along the route with food, drinks, and energy supplies. Refreshment point at the top of the Kladje climb.
vitals.cutoff_time: Road closures enforced — Godovic-Idrija closed 9:40-13:05, Cerkno-Kladje-Trebija closed 10:40-15:00
distance_options: Gran Fondo: 156 km (97 mi), 2,822 m (9,258 ft) elevation gain
Medio Fondo: 97 km (60 mi), 1,625 m (5,331 ft) elevation gain

climate.description: Mid-June in Ljubljana and the surrounding countryside brings warm, generally pleasant cycling conditions. Average temperatures of 20C with highs reaching 25-28C and lows around 14C. The Ljubljana Basin sits at 300m elevation with the course climbing to a maximum of 801m, so temperature variation along the route is modest. June is one of the wetter months in Slovenia with approximately 125mm of precipitation spread across 13 days, meaning afternoon thunderstorms are a real possibility.
challenges: Afternoon thunderstorms can develop — June is Ljubljana's wettest month
Warm conditions (25-28C) on exposed road sections demand steady hydration
Descents after the Kladje climb can feel cool if wet weather moves in
Long daylight hours (15+ hours) are an advantage, not a challenge

terrain.primary: Paved Slovenian provincial and back-country roads
terrain.surface: All paved throughout. Slovenian provincial roads range from well-maintained primary routes to narrower back-country lanes. The descent from Kladje includes sections with rough road paving. One short macadam (packed gravel) section near Povodje approaching the finish.
features: Ljubljana Basin: flat departure through the capital before climbing begins
Vrhnika Hill: 4.6 km at 4.1% average, 7% maximum — the opener
Logatec Plateau: rolling terrain through the Karst region
Godovic descent to Idrija: dangerous, technical descent — caution required
Kladje Climb: 7 km at 6.3% average, 10% maximum — the crux of the race
Kladje descent: 10 km, rough road surface, dangerous — the most technical section
Skofja Loka to Vodice: rolling terrain through medieval town
Final approach: flat run through Gameljne back to BTC City Ljubljana

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: One of Europe's oldest continuously running gran fondos (since 1982). Founding member of the UCI Gran Fondo World Series. Named after a WWII partisan hospital. 150,000+ lifetime finishers from 80+ countries. A Slovenian national institution.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: prestige=4 for founding UCI GF World Series membership and 43+ unbroken editions since 1982. logistics=4 because Ljubljana is a capital city with its own international airport 26 km away. altitude=1 at max 801m — no meaningful altitude challenge. elevation=3 for 2,822m of total climbing across two significant climbs. value=4 and expenses=4 reflect Ljubljana's affordability and the EUR 44-55 entry fee for a fully supported UCI gran fondo.

logistics.explanation: Ljubljana is a small, navigable European capital with its own international airport (LJU) just 26 km from the city center. The start/finish at BTC City is well-connected by public transport. Registration is open — no lottery, no ballot, no waitlist. Sign up, pay EUR 44-55, show up. Packet pickup is straightforward. Ljubljana's compact size means everything is close. Alternative airports in Venice (4 hours), Vienna (3.5 hours), or Munich (5 hours) offer more flight options. The only friction is that LJU has limited international routes.

length.explanation: 156 km (97 miles) for the Gran Fondo is a proper full-day endurance effort. Not an ultra, but not a casual century either. The 2,822m of climbing spread across the distance means you are constantly working — the flat sections are relatively brief. The Medio Fondo at 97 km provides a genuine alternative that is not a consolation prize. Average finishing times for the Gran Fondo run 5-7 hours depending on fitness. This is a distance that demands respect and preparation.

technicality.explanation: All paved throughout. This is a road cycling event on Slovenian provincial roads. The surfaces range from good to excellent on the main routes, with a caveat: the 10 km descent from Kladje has rough road paving that demands attention. There is a short macadam section near Povodje approaching the finish. Bring a standard road bike with appropriate gearing for the climbs. Technical demands are limited to descending on sometimes-narrow roads with 3,000 other riders.

elevation.explanation: 2,822 meters of total elevation gain across 156 km. The climbing is concentrated in two main efforts: the Vrhnika Hill (4.6 km at 4.1%) as a warm-up and the Kladje climb (7 km at 6.3%, max 10%) as the defining test. The rest is rolling terrain through the Ljubljana Basin and Julian Alps foothills. This is not an Alpine epic — there are no 2,000m passes — but the accumulated climbing is honest and relentless. The course descends slowly from the Kladje summit at km 90 all the way to the finish.

climate.explanation: Mid-June in Ljubljana is about as pleasant as cycling weather gets. Average 20C, highs of 25-28C, lows around 14C. Long daylight hours. The climate challenge is modest — this is not a heat event, not a cold event, not an extreme weather event. The caveat is that June is Ljubljana's wettest month. Afternoon thunderstorms can develop over the hills with reasonable frequency. But for most riders in most years, the weather is a non-issue.

altitude.explanation: Maximum course elevation: 801 meters above sea level. The start in Ljubljana is at approximately 300m. There is zero meaningful altitude effect. No rider from any background will experience altitude-related performance issues. This is a low-elevation event by any standard.

adventure.explanation: The route loops through genuinely attractive Slovenian countryside — the Ljubljana Basin, the foothills approaching the Julian Alps, the historic mercury-mining town of Idrija, the medieval center of Skofja Loka. The passage near the Franja Partisan Hospital adds a layer of historical depth that is unique among gran fondos. But this is not wilderness riding. The roads pass through populated areas, the scenery is pastoral rather than dramatic, and the terrain is pretty rather than jaw-dropping.

prestige.explanation: One of the oldest continuously running gran fondos in Europe, organized annually since 1982. Founding member of the UCI Gran Fondo World Series when it launched in 2011 — Maraton Franja has been part of the series from day one. Over 150,000 cyclists from 80+ countries have crossed the finish line. In Slovenian cycling culture, Franja is THE event — a national institution. Internationally recognized and respected, though it does not carry the cachet of Italian monuments like Maratona dles Dolomites or Nove Colli.

race_quality.explanation: 43+ editions of continuous organizational refinement. UCI-sanctioned with full timing, age-group categories, and World Championship qualifying. Closed roads on key sections. Multiple refreshment stations stocked with food and drinks. Medical support along the route. The multi-day format — time trial Friday, family marathon Saturday, gran fondo Sunday — creates a genuine cycling festival. Road closures are tightly managed with specific windows. The organization reflects over four decades of experience.

experience.explanation: The ride delivers a genuine tour of Slovenia's heartland. You leave Ljubljana's urban sprawl, climb into rolling hills, descend through the historic mercury town of Idrija, tackle the Kladje climb through alpine forest, drop into the medieval streets of Skofja Loka, and roll back into the capital. The WWII partisan hospital heritage — the event's namesake — adds emotional weight that most gran fondos lack. Ljubljana itself is a charming, walkable capital city. The overall experience is warm, welcoming, and distinctly Slovenian.

community.explanation: 150,000+ lifetime finishers from 80+ countries, including 23,000+ women. The event draws 3,000 riders from 31+ countries each year — impressive for an event in a small European country of 2 million people. The Slovenian cycling community treats Franja as a national celebration. The family marathon on Saturday brings thousands more casual participants. The atmosphere is inclusive, warm, and deeply rooted in Slovenian sporting identity. This is not just a race — it is a cultural event.

field_depth.explanation: As a UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier, the front of the field attracts competitive age-group racers aiming for World Championship slots. Slovenian club riders push hard at the pointy end. But the majority of the 3,000-rider field is recreational — riding for the experience, the medal, the personal challenge. The field depth is moderate: competitive at the top, participatory through the middle and back. International representation is broad but not deep in any single age category.

value.explanation: EUR 44 early bird (EUR 55 regular) for a UCI-sanctioned gran fondo with closed roads, full support, refreshment stations, a finisher medal, lunch at the finish, and a gift package. This is exceptional value by European gran fondo standards — compare to EUR 80-180 for comparable events in Italy, France, or Spain. The entry fee alone makes Franja one of the best-value UCI qualifiers in the world.

expenses.explanation: Ljubljana is one of Europe's most affordable capital cities. Hotel rooms in the city center run EUR 60-120/night. AirBnB options are plentiful and cheaper. Meals in Ljubljana are EUR 8-20. The airport is 26 km away with EUR 4 bus connections. Car rental is optional — the city is compact and cycling-friendly. Budget EUR 400-800 for a long weekend from within Europe. From the US, budget EUR 1,000-1,500 including flights. Slovenia punches well above its weight on the affordability scale.

biased_opinion.summary: Maraton Franja is not the hardest gran fondo, not the most scenic, not the most famous. What it is, without qualification, is one of the most genuine. Running since 1982 — before 'gran fondo' was even a recognized category — this Slovenian institution has quietly built a 43-year legacy of accessible, well-organized road cycling through the Ljubljana Basin and Julian Alps foothills. Named after a secret WWII partisan hospital hidden in a mountain gorge, the event carries historical weight that no Italian monument or French classic can replicate. The Kladje climb will test you. The descent will scare you. The finish in Ljubljana will satisfy you. And the EUR 44 entry fee will make you wonder why everyone else charges three times as much.
strengths: 43+ continuous editions since 1982 — one of Europe's oldest gran fondos
UCI Gran Fondo World Series founding member with World Championship qualifying
Exceptional value: EUR 44-55 for a fully supported, UCI-sanctioned event
Ljubljana is affordable, accessible, and genuinely charming as a host city
weaknesses: Maximum elevation of 801m means no Alpine drama — this is a foothills event
The 10 km descent from Kladje has rough road surface requiring caution
International name recognition lags behind Italian and French gran fondos
June rain risk: Ljubljana's wettest month brings afternoon thunderstorm potential
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want a UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier that does not require a second mortgage or a lottery win, Maraton Franja is the answer. Four decades of Slovenian organizational pride, a meaningful historical connection, and a EUR 44 entry fee that includes lunch. The climbs are honest, the countryside is beautiful, and the Slovenian cycling community will make you feel welcome. This is gran fondo cycling as it was meant to be — unpretentious, affordable, and built to last.

final_verdict.one_liner: Europe's oldest gran fondo. 43 editions since 1982. UCI World Series founding member. EUR 44 entry. The Slovenian institution that proves longevity beats hype.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want a well-organized UCI gran fondo qualifier at a fraction of the cost of Italian or French alternatives. Yes if the historical connection to a WWII partisan hospital resonates. Yes if you want to discover Slovenia — an underrated European cycling destination. Skip if you need Alpine drama — the maximum elevation is 801m. Skip if you need a globally recognized name on your cycling resume.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a comparable UCI qualifier with more climbing: La Marmotte (France). If you want European heritage without the Alps: Nove Colli (Italy). If you want another Central European UCI gran fondo: Tour de Pologne Amatorow (Poland).

logistics.airport: Ljubljana Joze Pucnik Airport (LJU) — 26 km from city center, 30-40 min by bus (EUR 4) or taxi. Limited international routes but growing. Alternative airports: Venice Marco Polo (VCE) — 4 hours. Vienna (VIE) — 3.5 hours. Munich (MUC) — 5 hours. Zagreb (ZAG) — 2 hours.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Ljubljana city center — compact, walkable, and well-connected to the BTC City start/finish by public transport or a short ride. Hotels EUR 60-120/night, Airbnb options plentiful. The city center is charming with excellent restaurants and cafes. No need for a car — Ljubljana is one of Europe's most cycling-friendly capitals. For a quieter option, stay in Skofja Loka (on the course, 30 min from Ljubljana) for a medieval town atmosphere.
logistics.food: Slovenian cuisine is a crossroads of Italian, Austrian, and Balkan influences. Struklji (rolled dumplings), potica (nut roll), jota (bean stew), local wines from Goriska Brda. Ljubljana's restaurant scene is excellent and affordable. Carb loading is effortless. Feed zones on the course provide food, drinks, and energy supplies at multiple points including the Kladje summit.
logistics.packet_pickup: Number pickup in Ljubljana, typically 1-2 days before at the event venue. Bring ID and confirmation email. Well-organized with decades of experience handling thousands of international participants.
logistics.parking: BTC City has ample parking — it is a large commercial center. Follow event-specific parking instructions. Public transport from Ljubljana center is convenient.

history.founder: Slovenian cycling community organizers in Ljubljana
history.origin_story: The first Maraton Franja took place on July 22, 1982, with approximately 700 participants — most on regular, non-competitive bicycles. The event was named after the Franja Partisan Hospital, a secret WWII medical facility hidden in the Pasice gorge near Cerkno. Built in December 1943, the hospital was managed by Dr. Franja Bojc Bidovec and treated wounded partisans and Allied forces in complete secrecy, protected by retractable bridges, minefields, and camouflaged buildings. The marathon route passes near this UNESCO-nominated site, paying tribute to the courage of its medical staff. From those 700 inaugural riders, the event grew steadily through Slovenian independence (1991), reached record participation of 1,886 in 2002, and was selected as a founding member of the UCI Gran Fondo World Series in 2011. Over 150,000 cyclists from 80+ countries have completed the event.
notable_moments: 1982: Inaugural edition — July 22, approximately 700 participants on mostly non-competitive bicycles
1991: Continued through Slovenian independence — the event predates the nation itself
2002: Record participation of 1,886 in the main marathon, plus first Family Marathon
2011: Selected as founding member of the UCI Gran Fondo World Series
Lifetime: 150,000+ finishers from 80+ countries, including 23,000+ women
history.reputation: The quiet elder of European gran fondos. Not the flashiest, not the hardest, but one of the most enduring — running since before Slovenia was even an independent country. A founding pillar of the UCI Gran Fondo World Series and a Slovenian national cycling institution.

course_description.character: A 156 km loop through the Ljubljana Basin and Julian Alps foothills. The route leaves Ljubljana, climbs gently over the Vrhnika Hill, traverses the Logatec Plateau, descends through the historic mercury-mining town of Idrija, then tackles the defining Kladje climb — 7 km at 6.3% average with a 10% maximum pitch. After a long, rough descent through alpine forest, the route passes through medieval Skofja Loka before rolling back to Ljubljana across pastoral terrain. The course is diverse: urban start, rolling plateau, alpine forest climbs, historic towns, and flat agricultural finish. Maximum elevation is 801m — this is a foothills event, not an Alpine epic.

suffering_zones.label: Ljubljana to Vrhnika — The Warm-Up
suffering_zones.desc: Flat departure from BTC City through Ljubljana's outskirts before the first climb begins. The peloton thins out naturally as riders find their pace. Urban roads give way to the Vrhnika Hill — a 4.6 km opener at 4.1% average, 7% maximum. Not steep enough to cause damage but steep enough to remind you what is coming.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Urban roads, then 4.6 km climb at 4.1% average

suffering_zones.label: Logatec Plateau to Idrija — Rolling and Descending
suffering_zones.desc: The route crosses the Logatec Plateau — rolling terrain through the Slovenian Karst region — before turning toward Godovic and dropping into the Idrija valley. The descent from Godovic to Idrija is flagged as dangerous: steep, technical, and demanding attention. Idrija itself is a UNESCO-listed mercury mining town with centuries of history.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling plateau, then dangerous technical descent into Idrija

suffering_zones.label: Cerkno to Kladje — The Crux
suffering_zones.desc: After passing through Cerkno and the valley near the Franja Partisan Hospital site, the road tilts upward for the defining climb. Kladje is 7 km at 6.3% average with a maximum gradient of 10%. This is the hardest sustained effort of the day — not extreme by Alpine standards, but honest and relentless. A refreshment point at the summit provides critical refueling.

suffering_zones.label: Kladje Descent to Skofja Loka — The Dangerous Descent
suffering_zones.desc: From the Kladje summit, a 10 km descent begins that is notorious for rough road paving. The organizers flag this as dangerous, and with good reason — bad surfaces at speed with tired legs demand concentration. The route then enters medieval Skofja Loka, one of Slovenia's best-preserved old towns, before turning toward Vodice and the final run home.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 10 km descent with rough road surface, then rolling terrain

suffering_zones.label: Vodice to Ljubljana — The Run Home
suffering_zones.desc: From Skofja Loka through Vodice and Gameljne, the course descends gently and flattens for the final approach to BTC City. A short macadam section near Povodje adds texture before the Crnuski bridge crossing and the run along Smartinska cesta to the finish. The last 25 km are mentally easy but physically draining on empty legs.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Gently descending, mostly flat, one short macadam section

course_description.signature_challenge: The Kladje climb: 7 km at 6.3% average with a maximum gradient of 10%. It arrives at approximately km 90 after the plateau crossings and the Idrija valley, on legs that have already absorbed 60+ km of rolling terrain. The climb itself is honest — no false flats, no respite sections. But the real test comes immediately after: a 10 km descent on rough road surface that demands concentration when you most want to coast.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Slovenian provincial roads — Ljubljana Basin loop through Julian Alps foothills
guide_variables.race_weather: Mid-June continental — warm (20-28C), possible afternoon thunderstorms, long daylight hours
race_challenges: 2,822m of total climbing across 156 km with two significant climbs
Kladje climb: 7 km at 6.3% average, 10% maximum — the crux at km 90
Dangerous descents: Godovic to Idrija and 10 km from Kladje on rough road
June thunderstorm risk in the Slovenian hills

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained climbing endurance — multiple 20-40 minute climbs in a single long ride
non_negotiables.why: The Kladje climb is 7 km at 6.3% — roughly 25-35 minutes of sustained effort depending on fitness. But it arrives after 90 km of rolling terrain. Train with long rides (120+ km) that include multiple climbs. Your legs need to produce power on climb number three after burning matches on climbs one and two.

non_negotiables.requirement: Technical descending confidence on imperfect surfaces
non_negotiables.why: The 10 km descent from Kladje has rough road paving — the organizers flag it as dangerous for good reason. At 40-60 km/h on tired legs with 3,000 other riders, you need to be comfortable descending on surfaces that are not smooth. Practice descending on secondary roads, not just freshly paved highways.

non_negotiables.requirement: Full race simulation — 140+ km with 2,500m+ climbing and nutrition plan
non_negotiables.why: 156 km with 2,822m of climbing burns 3,500-5,000 calories over 5-7 hours. Your nutrition strategy must sustain steady power output through the Kladje climb at km 90. Practice eating and drinking on a ride that genuinely fatigues you. The summit refreshment station is critical — rehearse refueling at effort.
